checks.question: In parallel reduction Version 1 (interleaved addressing with divergent branching), why does the condition `if (i % (2 * stride) == 0)` cause warp divergence, and how does Version 2 fix it?
options: Version 1 causes divergence because even and odd threads in the same warp take different branches; Version 2 eliminates divergence by using sequential thread IDs (0..stride-1) so that all active threads are contiguous and fill complete warps
Version 1 causes bank conflicts, not divergence; Version 2 uses padding to fix this
Both versions have the same divergence; Version 2 is faster because it uses fewer registers
Version 1 is actually divergence-free; the improvement in Version 2 comes from better memory coalescing
checks.explanation: In Version 1, at stride = 1, the condition `i % 2 == 0` means threads 0, 2, 4, ... are active while threads 1, 3, 5, ... are idle. Within warp 0, threads 0 and 1 take different paths -- classic warp divergence. In Version 2, threads 0, 1, 2, ..., stride-1 are active (contiguous). At stride = n/2, the first n/2 threads are all active, filling complete warps with no divergence. Only the boundary warp (if n/2 is not a multiple of 32) has any divergence. The key insight: arrange active threads contiguously so that within each warp, either all threads are active or all are inactive.

checks.question: The Hillis-Steele scan completes in $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ steps but performs $O(n \log n)$ total work. The Blelloch scan performs $O(n)$ total work but takes $2\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ steps. When is each algorithm preferred on a GPU?
options: Hillis-Steele is always preferred because fewer steps means lower latency
Blelloch is always preferred because it does less total work

Hillis-Steele is preferred when the GPU has many more processors than elements (latency-limited regime); Blelloch is preferred when work efficiency matters more (throughput-limited regime, large arrays)
The two algorithms have identical performance on GPUs because the GPU has enough parallelism to hide the extra work
checks.explanation: This is a classic work-depth trade-off. Hillis-Steele has lower depth ($\log n$ steps) but higher work ($n \log n$ operations). Blelloch has higher depth ($2 \log n$ steps) but optimal work ($O(n)$). On a GPU with P processors and an array of N elements: if $P \gg N$ (more processors than elements), the extra work of Hillis-Steele is free because processors would be idle anyway, and the lower step count wins. If $P \ll N$ (common case: millions of elements), the extra work of Hillis-Steele wastes processor cycles, and Blelloch's work efficiency dominates. In practice, large-scale GPU scans use Blelloch within blocks and a hierarchical approach across blocks.

checks.question: In tiled matrix multiplication with TILE_SIZE = 16, the naive implementation reads $2 \times M \times N \times K$ floats from global memory. The tiled version reads approximately $2 \times M \times N \times K / \text{TILE\_SIZE}$ floats. For a $4096 \times 4096$ matrix multiply, how many global memory reads does tiling save?
options: The tiled version saves no reads; it just changes the access pattern
The tiled version reads $2 \times 4096^3 / 16 \approx 8.6$ billion floats instead of $2 \times 4096^3 \approx 137$ billion, saving ~93.75% of reads
The tiled version reads from shared memory instead of global memory, but the total data transferred is the same
Tiling only helps with bank conflicts, not with the total amount of data read
checks.explanation: Naive: $2 \times 4096^3 = 2 \times 68{,}719{,}476{,}736 \approx 137.4$ billion float reads from global memory. Tiled with TILE_SIZE=16: $2 \times 4096^3 / 16 \approx 8.6$ billion reads. The reduction is exactly TILE_SIZE = 16x. Each tile of data is loaded once from global memory into shared memory and reused TILE_SIZE times by different threads. At 4 bytes per float, this means the tiled version transfers ~34.4 GB vs ~549.8 GB from global memory -- the difference between being compute-bound (achieving most of the H100's 989 TFLOPS TF32) and being hopelessly memory-bound. This reuse factor is the fundamental reason tiled GEMM dominates GPU computing.

checks.question: In Ring-AllReduce with $N = 8$ GPUs and an array of $K$ elements per GPU, each GPU sends a total of $2(N-1)/N \times K = 2 \times 7/8 \times K = 1.75K$ data. If $K = 1$ billion float32 values (4 GB) and each GPU's NVLink bandwidth is 900 GB/s (H100), what is the minimum time for the AllReduce?
options: 7.8 ms (1.75 x 4 GB / 900 GB/s, accounting for the total data each GPU must send)
3.1 ms (send and receive are pipelined, so effective bandwidth is doubled)
35 ms (each of the 14 steps must complete before the next starts)
checks.explanation: Each GPU sends $1.75K = 1.75 \times 4 = 7$ GB of data total. At 900 GB/s NVLink bandwidth (H100): $7 / 900 \approx 7.8$ ms. This is the bandwidth-optimal lower bound -- no AllReduce algorithm can do better. The critical insight: the communication volume per GPU is approximately $2K$ regardless of $N$ (for large $N$, $2(N-1)/N \approx 2$). Doubling the number of GPUs does not double the communication time per GPU. This is why Ring-AllReduce scales well: going from 8 to 64 GPUs increases per-GPU communication from $1.75K$ to $1.97K$ -- only a 12.5% increase despite 8x more GPUs.
